aWeek 2
1. Set Notations 2. De Morgan's Law 3. Symbols 4. Distance & Norm 5. Metric Space 6. Sequences 7. Graph 8. Continuity and Differentiability 9. Linear Approximation 10. Quadratic Approximation 11. Product Rule 12. Chain Rule 13. Local Maxima, Local Minima, Saddle Point 14. Geometry of Lines 15. Directional Derivative, Partial Derivative, Gradient 16. Direction of Steepest Ascent, Descent and No Change 17. Linear Approximation (Multivariable Functions) 18. Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality 19. Triangular Inequality 20. Level Curves and Contour Plots
1. Set Notations• RR → Set of all real numbers• R+R+ → Set of all positive real numbers, including 0• ZZ → Set of all integers• Z+Z+ → Set of all positive integers, including 0• [a,b] = {x∈R| a⩽x⩽b}[a,b]={x∈R|a⩽x⩽b}• (a,b) = {x∈R| a<x<b}(a,b)={x∈R|a<x<b}• Rd = R×R×...d-times....×RRd=R×R×...d-times....×R• [a,b]d = {x×Rda xi∈[a,b], i∈{1,2,...,d}}[a,b]d={x×Rd|xi∈[a,b],i∈{1,2,...,d}} Example : [-2,2]2[-2,2]2
 We can also write [-2,2]2[-2,2]2 as a cartesian product : [-2,2]×[-2,-2][-2,2]×[-2,-2]
2. De Morgan's LawLet AA & BB be two sets. Then the following are true(A∪B)C=AC∩BC (A∩B)C=AC∪BC(A∪B)C=AC∩BC(A∩B)C=AC∪BC
3. SymbolsFor All : ∀∀ & There exist : ∃∃Without SymbolIf VV is a vector space, then for all u∈Vu∈V, there exists a u'∈Vu'∈V such that u+u'=v0u+u'=v0, where v0v0 is the zero vector of VV. With SymbolIf VV is a vector space, then ∀u∈V, ∃u'∈V∀u∈V,∃u'∈V such that u+u'=v0u+u'=v0, where v0v0 is the zero vector of VV.Implies : ⟹⟹• x=3⟹x2=9x=3⟹x2=9Equivalent : ⟺⟺• nn is divisible by 6 ⟺⟺ nn is divisibe by 2 and 3
4. Distance & NormNote : In this section, we will use the word point and vector interchangably Let v,u∈Rdv,u∈Rd where v=(v1,v2,...,vd)v=(v1,v2,...,vd) and u=(u1,u2,...,ud)u=(u1,u2,...,ud).Manhattan DistanceThe Manhattan Distance between two points vv and uu is given by:d∑i=1|vi-ui|=|v1-u1|+|v2-u2|+...+|vd-ud|∑i=1d|vi-ui|=|v1-u1|+|v2-u2|+...+|vd-ud|
|v1-u1||v1-u1||v2-u2||v2-u2| Euclidean DistanceThe Euclidean Distance between two points uu and vv is given byd∑i=1(vi-ui)2=(v1-u1)2+(v2-u2)2+...+(vd-ud)2∑i=1d(vi-ui)2=(v1-u1)2+(v2-u2)2+...+(vd-ud)2
l1l1 NormThe l1l1 norm (also known as the Manhattan norm) of vector vv is given by‖v‖1=|v1|+|v2|+...+|vd|‖v‖1=|v1|+|v2|+...+|vd|l2l2 NormThe l2l2 norm (also known as the Euclidean norm) of vector uu is given by‖u‖2=u21+u22+...+u2d=uTu‖u‖2=u12+u22+...+ud2=uTu (here, we assume uu is a column vector, therefore, uTuT will be a row vector)
5. Metric SpaceA metric space is an ordered pair : (M,d)(M,d), where:• MM is a set• d:M×M→Rd:M×M→R is a distance or metric function Suppose x,y∈Mx,y∈M, and 𝜖∈R𝜖∈R, we define B(x,𝜖)B(x,𝜖) and ⏨B(x,𝜖)B¯(x,𝜖) as follows:• B(x,𝜖)=ayad(x,y)<𝜖aB(x,𝜖)={y|d(x,y)<𝜖}• ⏨B(x,𝜖)=ayad(x,y)⩽𝜖aB¯(x,𝜖)={y|d(x,y)⩽𝜖} ExampleLet (R2,d)(R2,d) be a metric space where,d(x,y)=‖x-y‖2d(x,y)=‖x-y‖2
Ba(0,0),2a=a(x,y)∈R2ax2+y2<2aB((0,0),2)={(x,y)∈R2|x2+y2<2}⏨Ba(0,0),2a=a(x,y)∈R2ax2+y2⩽2aB¯((0,0),2)={(x,y)∈R2|x2+y2⩽2}ExampleLet (R2,d)(R2,d) be a metric space where,d(x,y)=‖x-y‖1d(x,y)=‖x-y‖1
Ba(0,0),2a=a(x,y)∈R2a|x|+|y|<2aB((0,0),2)={(x,y)∈R2||x|+|y|<2}⏨Ba(0,0),2a=a(x,y)∈R2a|x|+|y|⩽2aB¯((0,0),2)={(x,y)∈R2||x|+|y|⩽2}
6. SequencesA sequence is a list of numbers in a given order.a1,a2,...,an,...a1,a2,...,an,... Sequences can be described by writing rules that specify their terms.an=n-1
nan=n-1nor, by listing the terms:{an}={0,1
2,2
3,3
4,...}{an}={0,12,23,34,...} A sequence {an}{an} converges to LL if for every positive number 𝜖𝜖 there corresponds an integer NN such that|an-L|<𝜖
whenever
 n⩾N|an-L|<𝜖whenevern⩾N If no such number LL exists, we say that {an}{an} diverges. If {an}{an} converges to LL, we write limn→∞an=Llimn→∞an=L, or simply an→Lan→L. and call LL the limit of the sequence. Look at the diagram below. We take two epsilons : 𝜖1𝜖1 and 𝜖2𝜖2, where 𝜖1>𝜖2>0𝜖1>𝜖2>0. Similarly, no matter how small epsilon becomes (as long as it is greater than 0), we should be able to find NN, such that, for all n⩾Nn⩾N, |an-L|<𝜖|an-L|<𝜖.
LLL+𝜖1L+𝜖1L-𝜖1L-𝜖1NN
LLL+𝜖2L+𝜖2L-𝜖2L-𝜖2NN
7. GraphConsider a function f:Rd→Rf:Rd→R Graph of ff, denoted by GfGf is a subset of Rd+1Rd+1.Gf={(x,f(x))ax∈Rd}Gf={(x,f(x))|x∈Rd} Suppose x∈R2x∈R2, then (x,f(x))=(x1,x2,f(x))(x,f(x))=(x1,x2,f(x))
8. Continuity and DifferentiabilityContinuityA function f:R→Rf:R→R is continuous at x*∈Rx*∈R, if for all an→x*an→x*, f(an)→f(x*)f(an)→f(x*). Consider the following function:f(x)=aa
|x|
x		, if x≠0
0, if x=0
f(x)={|x|x,ifx≠00,ifx=0 Consider the following sequence:an=1
nan=1n We can see that an→0an→0. However, f(an)→1f(an)→1. Therefore, ff is not continuous at 00. Also, if we consider the following sequence,bn=-1
nbn=-1nwe see that bn→0bn→0 and f(bn)→-1f(bn)→-1. Both anan and bnbn converge to 00. But, f(an)→1f(an)→1 and f(bn)→-1f(bn)→-1.
DifferentiabilityA function f:R→Rf:R→R is differentiable at x∈Rx∈R, if the following limit exist:limh→0f(x+h)-f(x)
(x+h)-(x)limh→0f(x+h)-f(x)(x+h)-(x)
hhxx As h→0h→0, limh→0f(x+h)-f(x)
(x+h)-(x)limh→0f(x+h)-f(x)(x+h)-(x) gives the instantaneous rate of change of the ff at xx.
9. Linear ApproximationLet f:R→Rf:R→R be a function differentiable at c∈Rc∈R. The linear approximation for ff at cc is given by:f(x)≈f(c)+f'(c)(x-c)f(x)≈f(c)+f'(c)(x-c) Informal proof:f'(c)=limx→cf(x)-f(c)
x-cf'(c)=limx→cf(x)-f(c)x-cwhen xx is "close" to c,f'(c)≈f(x)-f(c)
x-cf'(c)≈f(x)-f(c)x-c f(x)≈f(c)+f'(c)(x-c)f(x)≈f(c)+f'(c)(x-c) In simple words, when xx is close to cc, we can linearly approximate the value of f(x)f(x) evaluating f(c)+f'(c)(x-c)f(c)+f'(c)(x-c). Consider f(x)=(0.2)x2f(x)=(0.2)x2. The linear apporximation for ff at 55 is given by: f(x)≈f(5)+f'(5)(x-5) f(x)≈(0.2×25)+(0.2×10)(x-5) f(x)≈5+2x-10 f(x)≈2x-5f(x)≈f(5)+f'(5)(x-5)f(x)≈(0.2×25)+(0.2×10)(x-5)f(x)≈5+2x-10f(x)≈2x-5
10. Quadratic ApproximationLet f:R→Rf:R→R be a function differentiable at x=cx=c. The quadratic approximation for ff at xx is given by:f(x)≈f(c)+f'(c)(x-c)+1
2f''(c)(x-c)2f(x)≈f(c)+f'(c)(x-c)+12f''(c)(x-c)2 Consider f(x)=x3-3x+1f(x)=x3-3x+1. The quadratic approximation for ff at -1-1 is given as follows
f(x)≈f(-1)+f'(-1)(x-(-1))+1
2f''(-1)(x-(-1)2
≈3+f'(-1)(x+1)+1
2f''(-1)(x+1)2
f(x)≈f(-1)+f'(-1)(x-(-1))+12f''(-1)(x-(-1)2≈3+f'(-1)(x+1)+12f''(-1)(x+1)2
f'(x)=3x2-3f''(x)=d
dx(f'(x))
f'(-1)=3-3f''(x)=d
dx(3x2-3)
f'(-1)=0f''(x)=6x
f''(-1)=-6
f'(x)=3x2-3f''(x)=ddx(f'(x))f'(-1)=3-3f''(x)=ddx(3x2-3)f'(-1)=0f''(x)=6xf''(-1)=-6
f(x)≈3+0(x+1)-(1
2)6(x2+2x+1)
≈3-3x2-6x-3
f(x)≈3+0(x+1)-(12)6(x2+2x+1)≈3-3x2-6x-3 Let us also find the linear approximation of ff at -1-1.
f(x)≈f(-1)+f'(-1)(x-(-1))
≈3+0(x+1)
≈3
f(x)≈f(-1)+f'(-1)(x-(-1))≈3+0(x+1)≈3Here, the blue curve is f(x)f(x), red line is its linear apporximation at -1-1 and green is its quadratic approximation at -1-1.
 We can see that the quadratic approximation is better than the linear approximation.
11. Product RuleLet f(x)=g(x)·h(x)f(x)=g(x)·h(x). Assume ff is differentiable at cc. f'(c)=g'(c)·h(c)+h'(c)·g(c)f'(c)=g'(c)·h(c)+h'(c)·g(c)
12. Chain RuleLet f(x)=paq(x)af(x)=p(q(x)). Assume: • pp is differentiable at q(x)q(x)• qq is differentiable at xx
f'(x)=limh→0f(x+h)-f(x)
h
=limh→0paq(x+h)a-paq(x)a
h
f'(x)=limh→0f(x+h)-f(x)h=limh→0p(q(x+h))-p(q(x))hMultiply and divide by q(x+h)-q(x)q(x+h)-q(x)
f'(x)=limh→0[paq(x+h)a-paq(x)a
q(x+h)-q(x)·q(x+h)-q(x)
h]
=limh→0paq(x+h)a-paq(x)a
q(x+h)-q(x)·limh→0q(x+h)-q(x)
h
f'(x)=limh→0[p(q(x+h))-p(q(x))q(x+h)-q(x)·q(x+h)-q(x)h]=limh→0p(q(x+h))-p(q(x))q(x+h)-q(x)·limh→0q(x+h)-q(x)h As h→0h→0, q(x+h)-q(x)→0q(x+h)-q(x)→0limh→0paq(x+h)a-paq(x)a
q(x+h)-q(x)=p'aq(x)alimh→0p(q(x+h))-p(q(x))q(x+h)-q(x)=p'(q(x)) Also,limh→0q(x+h)-q(x)
h=q'(x)limh→0q(x+h)-q(x)h=q'(x) Together,f'(x)=p'aq(x)a·q'(x)f'(x)=p'(q(x))·q'(x)
13. Local Maxima, Local Minima, Saddle PointConsider f:R→Rf:R→R • cc is a local minima of ff if there exists a real number 𝛼>0𝛼>0, such that for all x∈(c-𝛼,c+𝛼)x∈(c-𝛼,c+𝛼), f(c)⩽f(x)f(c)⩽f(x)• cc is a local maxima of ff if there exists a real number 𝛼>0𝛼>0, such that for all x∈(c-𝛼,c+𝛼)x∈(c-𝛼,c+𝛼), f(c)⩾f(x)f(c)⩾f(x)• cc is a critical point if f'(c)=0f'(c)=0 or f'(c)f'(c) does not exist• All local minimum and local maximum are critical points• A critical point which is neither a local minima nor a local maxima is a saddle point
AABBCC• A→A→Local Minima• B→B→Local Maxima• C→C→Saddle point
14. Geometry of LinesConsider u,v∈Rdu,v∈Rd. A line through uu, along the direction of vv is given byL1={x∈Rdax=u+kv, k∈R}L1={x∈Rd|x=u+kv,k∈R} The intuition for the above set can be seen in the diagram below. We can clearly see that the line LL follows parallelogram law of addition.
uuvvu+vu+vu+2vu+2vL1L12v2v A line through uu and vv is given by the following set:L2={x∈Rdax=u+k·(v-u), k∈R}L2={x∈Rd|x=u+k·(v-u),k∈R}
uuvv2(v-u)2(v-u)u+2(v-u)u+2(v-u)L2L2v-uv-u
HyperplaneLet b∈Rb∈R.Given a vector w∈Rdw∈Rd, we can describe a hyperplane which is normal to ww as follows: {x∈RdawTx=b}{x∈Rd|wTx=b}A hyperplane in RdRd is has dimension d-1d-1. Example in R2R2Let w=(2,3)w=(2,3). The hyperplane normal to ww is given by{(x,y)∈R2a2x+3y=b} {(x,y)∈R2|2x+3y=b}
 Example in R3R3Let w=(1,1,1)w=(1,1,1), k=(x,y,z)k=(x,y,z). The hyperplane normal to ww is given by{(x,y,z)∈R3ax+y+z=b} {(x,y,z)∈R3|x+y+z=b}
15. Directional Derivative, Partial Derivative, GradientDirectional DerivativeConsider a function f:R2→Rf:R2→R. Let v,u∈R2v,u∈R2 and let h∈Rh∈R.Here, uu is a unit vector. That is, ||u||=1||u||=1
uuvvh·uh·uv+h·uv+h·u From vv, if we happen to move hh units in the direction of uu, which is a unit vector, the function's value at the new point is f(v+hu)f(v+hu). At vv, the instantaneous rate of change of the function ff in the direction of uu is given by:Duf(v)=limh→0f(v+hu)-f(v)
hDuf(v)=limh→0f(v+hu)-f(v)hThe above limit, if it exists, is known as the directional derivative of ff at vv in the direction of the unit vector uu. Partial DerivativeIn R2R2, if uu happens to be:• (1,0)(1,0), we call it the partial derivative with respect to x1x1• (0,1)(0,1), we call it the partial derivative with respect to x2x2 In RnRn, let xi=(0,0,...,1....,0)xi=(0,0,...,1....,0), where 11 is in the ithith index, where i∈{1,2,....,n}i∈{1,2,....,n}. The directional derivative in the direction of xixi can also be called as the partial derivative with respect to xixi. It is given by𝛿f
𝛿xiav=Dxif(v)𝛿f𝛿xi|v=Dxif(v)A partial derivative measures how the function changes when we move only along one coordinate direction, keeping all other coordinates fixed.GradientLet f:Rn→Rf:Rn→R. Let v,u∈Rnv,u∈Rn and let ||u||=1||u||=1. The gradient of ff at vv is given by ∇f(v)=(𝛿f
𝛿x1av,⋯,𝛿f
𝛿xnav)∇f(v)=(𝛿f𝛿x1|v,⋯,𝛿f𝛿xn|v) In simple words, gradient vector is the collection of parital derivatives. We can compute the directional derivative of ff at vv in the direction of a unit vector uu as follows Duf(v)=∇f(v)·uDuf(v)=∇f(v)·u
16. Direction of Steepest Ascent, Descent and No ChangeCosine function's value at 0°, 90° & 180°0°,90°&180°
cos(0°)=1cos(90°)=0cos(180°)=-1
cos(0°)=1cos(90°)=0cos(180°)=-1 Note, the maximum value of the cosine function is 11 and the minimum value of the cosine function is -1-1.Angle between two unit vectorsLet p,q∈Rnp,q∈Rn. Suppose, the angle between pp and qq is 𝜃°𝜃°. Then, p
||p||·q
||q||=cos(𝜃°) = p·q=||p||×||q||×cos(𝜃°)p||p||·q||q||=cos(𝜃°)=p·q=||p||×||q||×cos(𝜃°)Few questionsConsider a function f:Rn→Rf:Rn→R. Let u,v∈Rnu,v∈Rn and let ||u||=1||u||=1. We know that the directional derivative of ff at vv in the direction of the unit vector uu is given by Duf(v)=∇f(v)·u Duf(v)=||∇f(v)||×||u||×cos(𝛼°) Duf(v)=||∇f(v)||×cos(𝛼°)Duf(v)=∇f(v)·uDuf(v)=||∇f(v)||×||u||×cos(𝛼°)Duf(v)=||∇f(v)||×cos(𝛼°) Here, 𝛼°𝛼° is the angle between ∇f(v)∇f(v) and uu. We can ask a few questions here:1. At the point vv, in which direction does the function increase most rapidly?2. At the point vv, in which direction does the function decrease most rapidly?3. At the point v v, in which direction does the function remain unchanged? At the point vv, in which direction does the function increase most rapidly?Here, we are essentially asking the following:• At the point vv, along which direction, is the directional derivative maximum? Duf(v)=||∇f(v)||×cos(𝛼°)Duf(v)=||∇f(v)||×cos(𝛼°) For the directional derivative to be maximum, we would want cos(𝛼°)cos(𝛼°) to be maximum. This happens when 𝛼°=0°𝛼°=0°. Therefore, when u=∇f(v)
||∇f(v)||u=∇f(v)||∇f(v)||, the directional derivative at vv is maximum. This direction is also called the direction of steepest ascent. At the point vv, in which direction does the function decrease most rapidly?Here, we are essentially asking the following:• At the point vv, along which direction, is the directional derivative minimum? Duf(v)=||∇f(v)||×cos(𝛼°)Duf(v)=||∇f(v)||×cos(𝛼°) For the directional derivative to be minimum, we would want cos(𝛼°)cos(𝛼°) to be minimum. This happens when 𝛼°=180°𝛼°=180°. Therefore, when u=-∇f(v)
||∇f(v)||u=-∇f(v)||∇f(v)||, the directional derivative at vv is minimum. This direction is also called the direction of steepest descent. At the point v v, in which direction does the function remain unchanged?Here, we are essentially asking the following:• At the point vv, along which direction, is the directional derivative 00? Duf(v)=||∇f(v)||×cos(𝛼°)Duf(v)=||∇f(v)||×cos(𝛼°) For the directional derivative to be 0, we would want cos(𝛼°)cos(𝛼°) to be maximum. This happens when 𝛼°=90°𝛼°=90°. Therefore, when ∇f(v)Tu=0∇f(v)Tu=0, the directional derivative at vv is 0.

2,1

2,1

18. Cauchy-Schwarz InequalityWe know the following-1⩽cos(𝜃°)⩽1-1⩽cos(𝜃°)⩽1 Let a,b∈Rna,b∈Rn and let 𝛼°𝛼° be the angle between aa and bb. Then,aTb
||a||·||b||=cos(𝛼°)aTb||a||·||b||=cos(𝛼°) Therefore,-1⩽aTb
||a||·||b||⩽1 ⇓ -||a||·||b||⩽aTb⩽||a||·||b||-1⩽aTb||a||·||b||⩽1⇓-||a||·||b||⩽aTb⩽||a||·||b|| This inequality is also known as the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality.
19. Triangular Inequalitya
||a+b||2=(a+b)T(a+b)
=aTa+2aTb+bTb
=||a||2+2aTb+||b||2
||a+b||2=(a+b)T(a+b)=aTa+2aTb+bTb=||a||2+2aTb+||b||2Rearanging terms:
||a+b||2-||a||2-||b||2
2		=aTb
||a+b||2-||a||2-||b||22=aTb We know that aTb⩽||a||·||b||aTb⩽||a||·||b||||a+b||2-||a||2-||b||2
2⩽||a||·||b|| ||a+b||2⩽||a||2+2·||a||·||b||+||b||2 ||a+b||2⩽(||a||+||b||)2||a+b||2-||a||2-||b||22⩽||a||·||b||||a+b||2⩽||a||2+2·||a||·||b||+||b||2||a+b||2⩽(||a||+||b||)2 Both sides are non-negative. Therefore,||a+b||2⩽(||a||+||b||)2 ||a+b||⩽||a||+||b||||a+b||2⩽(||a||+||b||)2||a+b||⩽||a||+||b||
20. Level Curves and Contour PlotsConsider a function f:R2→Rf:R2→R. For a constant c∈Rc∈R, the level curve corresponding to cc is the set{(x,y)af(x,y)=c}{(x,y)|f(x,y)=c}
f(x,y)=4f(x,y)=4f(x,y)=3f(x,y)=3f(x,y)=2f(x,y)=2f(x,y)=1f(x,y)=1 • S1={(x,y)af(x,y)=1}S1={(x,y)|f(x,y)=1}• S2={(x,y)af(x,y)=2}S2={(x,y)|f(x,y)=2}• S3={(x,y)af(x,y)=3}S3={(x,y)|f(x,y)=3}• S4={(x,y)af(x,y)=4}S4={(x,y)|f(x,y)=4}
S1S1S2S2S3S3S4S4CONTOUR PLOT In the picture below,• red region → function's value is positive• blue region → function's value is negative• white region → function's value is zero In the right side, we have the contour plot. Note that in some regions, the distance the adjacent level curves is comparatively small when compared other regions. • If the distance between adjacent level curves is small in a region, then in that region the function is changing rapidly. • If the distance between adjacent level curves is large in a region, then in that region the function is changing slowly.
The direction of the gradient vector at a point is normal to the tangent of the level curve at that point. This is because the gradient vector points in the direction in which the directional derivative at that point is maximum. On the other hand, the tangent direction to the level curve is a direction in which the function does not change. Therefore, the directional derivative in the tangent direction is zero. In the picture below, please note that it is not the gradient vector. It just represents the direction of the gradient vector at vv. We can see that the gradient's direction is perpendicular to the tangent of the level curve at vv
vv
